questions.questionText: A 4-year-old boy has poor eye contact, does not respond to his name, engages in repetitive hand-flapping, and insists on the same daily routine. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Autism Spectrum Disorder (impaired social communication + restricted/repetitive behaviors, onset in early developmental period)
questions.explanation: Core concept: ASD = deficits in social communication + restricted, repetitive behaviors. Why correct: DSM-5 ASD criteria: (1) persistent deficits in social communication/interaction (poor eye contact, impaired social reciprocity) + (2) restricted/repetitive patterns of behavior (stereotypies like hand-flapping, insistence on sameness, narrow interests). Onset in early development. Why A wrong: ADHD = inattention + hyperactivity/impulsivity WITHOUT social communication deficits or restricted interests. Why C wrong: Conduct disorder = rule-breaking, aggression, destruction — not social communication deficits. Why D wrong: Intellectual disability = below-average IQ (<70) + adaptive deficits — can coexist with ASD but separate. Clinical relevance: Early intervention (Applied Behavior Analysis/ABA) improves outcomes. Screen with M-CHAT at 18-24 months. Male:female ratio ~4:1. Exam trap: DSM-5: 'Autism Spectrum Disorder' replaced Asperger's, childhood disintegrative, and PDD-NOS (all now under one umbrella). Memory aid: 'ASD = Social deficit + Stereotyped behavior + early Development.' Reference: Ahuja – Child Psychiatry.
questions.conceptTag: ASD autism social communication stereotypy M-CHAT
